title: Stacks & Queues
description: Master C# Stack<T> and Queue<T> for LIFO/FIFO operations, valid parentheses, and BFS in coding interviews.

sections.title: Stack<T>: LIFO Operations
sections.explanation: Stack<T> provides Last-In-First-Out: `Push()` adds to top, `Pop()` removes and returns top, `Peek()` returns top without removing. `.Count` gives size. `Pop()` and `Peek()` throw on empty—check `.Count > 0` first. Stack is used for: matching brackets, undo operations, DFS, expression evaluation, and monotonic stack problems.

exercise.prompt: Create a Stack<string>, push "a", "b", "c", "d". Print the top element without removing it. Pop twice and print each popped value. Print the remaining count and the new top element.

validation.message: Peek: d, Pop: d, Pop: c, Count: 2, Peek: b.
Use Stack<string> with Push, Pop, and Peek.

exercise.hint: Push a, b, c, d. Peek returns d. First Pop returns d, second Pop returns c. Peek again returns b.

sections.title: Queue<T>: FIFO Operations
sections.explanation: Queue<T> provides First-In-First-Out: `Enqueue()` adds to back, `Dequeue()` removes and returns front, `Peek()` returns front without removing. Essential for BFS, task scheduling, and level-order traversal. `Dequeue()` and `Peek()` throw on empty—check `.Count > 0` first.

exercise.prompt: Simulate a print queue. Enqueue "report.pdf", "photo.jpg", "letter.doc". Print the first item in queue using Peek. Process (dequeue) two items, printing each. Print the remaining count and the next item to be processed.

validation.message: Peek: report.pdf, Dequeue: report.pdf, photo.jpg, Count: 1, Peek: letter.doc.
Use Queue<string> with Enqueue, Dequeue, and Peek.

exercise.hint: Enqueue all three. Peek shows first. Dequeue returns and removes from front.

sections.title: Valid Parentheses with Stack
sections.explanation: Classic interview problem: validate matching brackets using a Stack. For each opening bracket, push the expected closing bracket. For each closing bracket, check it matches the stack top. If the stack is empty at the end, all brackets are matched. This handles nested and multiple bracket types: (), [], {}.

exercise.prompt: Implement IsValid(string s) that checks if a string has valid matching parentheses (only round brackets for simplicity). Return true if valid. Test with "(())", "()()", "(()", ")(" and print each result.

validation.message: (()) and ()() are valid, (() and )( are invalid.
Use a Stack<char> with Push and Pop to match parentheses.

exercise.hint: For '(' push it. For ')' check stack is not empty and pop. Return stack.Count == 0.

sections.title: BFS with Queue
sections.explanation: Breadth-First Search uses a Queue to explore nodes level by level. Pattern: enqueue start, while queue not empty: dequeue current, process it, enqueue unvisited neighbors. Track visited nodes with a HashSet. BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs. For level-order processing, use `queue.Count` to batch each level.

exercise.prompt: Implement BFS level-by-level: for each level, print all nodes on one line separated by spaces. Use the graph: 0→{1,2}, 1→{3}, 2→{3,4}, 3→{}, 4→{}. Start from node 0. Print the level number before each level's nodes.

validation.message: Use Queue with level-by-level processing using queue.Count.

exercise.hint: while queue not empty: int size = queue.Count; loop size times to process one level. Increment level after.
